concepts.title: Answer every part of the question

questions.text: In a question that asks you to 'describe X and explain Y', a detailed answer to just one part can still earn full marks.
questions.explanation: In 2024, many students lost marks on multi-part questions (such as Q4c and Q5a.ii) by addressing only one part.
questions.studyTip: Tick off each command word as you answer it.

questions.text: A multi-part question only earns full marks when every part is addressed.
questions.explanation: Each distinct instruction in the question is worth marks, so all parts must be covered.
questions.studyTip: Two verbs in the question means two things to answer.

questions.text: When a question says 'explain', restating the information given in the stem is enough.
questions.explanation: Examiners stress that 'explain' requires extra information demonstrating knowledge, not repetition of the stem.
questions.studyTip: Explain = give the how/because, not just the what.

questions.text: An 'explain' answer must add reasoning or a mechanism beyond what the stem already provides.
questions.explanation: Marks come from the biological reasoning you add, not from copying the question.
questions.studyTip: If it's already in the question, it isn't your explanation.

concepts.title: Answer the context, not a pre-planned response

questions.text: Writing a well-rehearsed, pre-planned response on the topic is a safe way to guarantee marks.
questions.explanation: In 2024 and 2025, pre-planned or generic answers repeatedly failed because they did not address what was actually asked.
questions.studyTip: Answer THIS question, with ITS data and context.

questions.text: Answers must be tailored to the specific context of the question rather than reused from a generic response.
questions.explanation: Examiners repeatedly reward responses that engage with the exact scenario and stimulus material.
questions.studyTip: Read the scenario, then write to it specifically.

questions.text: If a question asks 'how' a process occurred, explaining 'why' the outcome happened is an acceptable substitute.
questions.explanation: In 2022, many students asked 'how speciation occurred' instead explained 'why the species are different' and lost marks.
questions.studyTip: How = the process. Why = the reason.

questions.text: A 'how' question asks for the process or mechanism, which is not the same as 'why' something is the case.
questions.explanation: Command words define what kind of answer earns marks, so match your response to the verb.
questions.studyTip: Underline the command word before you start.

questions.text: Stating two facts side by side is enough; the marker will infer the connection between them.
questions.explanation: In 2025, responses that stated 'chlorophyll increased' without connecting it to more light absorbed and more photosynthesis missed marks.
questions.studyTip: Show the chain: A, which causes B, therefore C.

questions.text: You must spell out the causal chain linking your statements rather than leave the connection implied.
questions.explanation: Markers award the reasoning that connects cause to effect, not a list of separate facts.
questions.studyTip: Join your ideas with 'because' and 'therefore'.

questions.text: If you run out of room, it is fine to keep writing in the margins or below the printed lines.
questions.explanation: In 2025, some students wrote below the prescribed lines instead of the 'Extra space for responses' pages, and in 2023 some flagged a continuation that was not there.
questions.studyTip: Out of room: use the extra-space pages at the back.

questions.text: Continue long answers on the extra-space pages and clearly note that you have done so.
questions.explanation: Markers need to find your full answer, so signpost any continuation accurately.
questions.studyTip: Write 'continued on extra-space page' and actually continue it.

questions.text: The terms accuracy, precision, repeatability and validity all describe roughly the same idea about good data.
questions.explanation: In 2024, these key-science-skills terms were 'often used in incorrect contexts'.
questions.studyTip: They are distinct - learn each definition.

questions.text: Accuracy, precision, repeatability and validity have distinct meanings and must be used in the correct context.
questions.explanation: Each term describes a different property of measurements or investigations.
questions.studyTip: Accuracy = close to true value; precision = measurements close together.

concepts.title: Describe HOW a variable was controlled

questions.text: Writing 'we controlled the amount of light' is a sufficient description of a controlled variable.
questions.explanation: In 2025, statements like 'providing the same amount of light' did not say how and simply restated the stem.
questions.studyTip: Name the method, not just the variable.

questions.text: A controlled variable should be described with how it was kept constant, not merely named.
questions.explanation: Examiners want the procedure that holds the variable steady across trials.
questions.studyTip: e.g. 'the same 60 W lamp at 30 cm for every trial'.

concepts.title: Limitations of the model vs the data

questions.text: When asked for a limitation of a scientific model, describing a limitation of the experimental data is acceptable.
questions.explanation: In 2024 and 2025, students gave data limitations when the model's limitations were required (e.g. the simulation oversimplified random trp variation).
questions.studyTip: Model limitation = what the model simplifies or leaves out.

questions.text: A limitation of a model concerns how it simplifies reality, which differs from limitations of the data.
questions.explanation: A model is an abstraction, so its limitations are about realism and assumptions.
questions.studyTip: Ask: what does this model fail to represent?

concepts.title: Justifications must match the choice made

questions.text: In a 'justify' answer, any biologically correct reasoning will score even if it does not match the data or option you chose.
questions.explanation: In 2025, some justifications did not match the student (X or Y) the response claimed to agree with.
questions.studyTip: Justify the exact choice you made.

questions.text: A justification must refer to the specific data, option or person it is meant to support.
questions.explanation: The reasoning only earns marks if it actually supports your stated choice.
questions.studyTip: Point back to the evidence for your answer.

concepts.title: Quote specific data to support a trend

questions.text: Describing a trend as 'the value increased' is enough when interpreting a graph or table.
questions.explanation: Data-analysis questions reward responses that quote and manipulate specific figures, not vague 'increased/decreased'.
questions.studyTip: Name numbers, units and what they represent.

questions.text: Trend descriptions should cite specific values from the data and state what they represent.
questions.explanation: Specific evidence shows you can read and use the data, not just eyeball it.
questions.studyTip: e.g. 'rose from 12 to 48 units between day 2 and day 6'.

questions.text: If antibody concentration starts rising on day 13, the individual must have been first exposed on day 13.
questions.explanation: In 2025, the antibody graph required recognising that exposure occurred before the rise, not on the same day.
questions.studyTip: A response takes time, so the cause comes first.

questions.text: A rise in antibody concentration occurs after exposure, so first exposure must precede the increase.
questions.explanation: Biological responses have a lag, which you must allow for when reading time-course graphs.
questions.studyTip: Trace back from the rise to find when exposure happened.

questions.text: Stating that a result 'did not match the control' is a complete justification on its own.
questions.explanation: In 2024, 'the banding pattern did not match the control' was not enough without further biological explanation.
questions.studyTip: Say WHY the result differs, biologically.

questions.text: A valid conclusion links the result to biological reasoning, not just a mismatch with the control.
questions.explanation: Markers want the underlying biology that explains the observed difference.
questions.studyTip: Connect the observation to a mechanism.

questions.text: Transcription is the process in which amino acids are joined together to form a polypeptide.
questions.explanation: In 2025, incorrect options described translation events when the question was about transcription.
questions.studyTip: Transcription = DNA to mRNA.

questions.text: Transcription produces mRNA from a DNA template; joining amino acids occurs in translation.
questions.explanation: Transcription and translation are separate stages with different products.
questions.studyTip: Translation = mRNA to protein.

questions.text: A codon and a nucleotide are the same thing - a single building block of mRNA.
questions.explanation: The 2023 report warned that 'codon' must not be confused with 'nucleotide' or 'base'.
questions.studyTip: Codon = three bases = one amino acid.

questions.text: A codon is a sequence of three mRNA bases that specifies one amino acid; a nucleotide is a single building block.
questions.explanation: These terms describe different levels of structure and must be used precisely.
questions.studyTip: Base = letter; nucleotide = base+sugar+phosphate; codon = 3 bases.

concepts.title: Losing a stop codon lengthens a protein

questions.text: A mutation that changes a stop codon into one coding for an amino acid would shorten the protein.
questions.explanation: In 2025, high-scoring answers explained that replacing a stop codon with a coding codon lets translation continue.
questions.studyTip: No stop signal means translation keeps going.

questions.text: If a stop codon mutates to code for an amino acid, translation continues and the polypeptide becomes longer.
questions.explanation: The ribosome only stops at a stop codon, so removing one extends the chain.
questions.studyTip: Lose the STOP, gain length.

questions.text: The proteome is the complete set of genes in an organism.
questions.explanation: In 2024, many students did not specifically describe the proteome as required.
questions.studyTip: Genome = genes; proteome = proteins.

questions.text: The proteome is the full set of proteins expressed by a cell, tissue or organism at a given time.
questions.explanation: Unlike the genome, the proteome changes with conditions and cell type.
questions.studyTip: Same genome, different proteome in different cells.

concepts.title: Secretory vesicles fuse with the plasma membrane

questions.text: During secretion, the transport vesicle fuses with the cell wall to release the protein.
questions.explanation: In 2024, many students incorrectly stated the vesicle fuses with the cell wall.
questions.studyTip: Vesicle fuses with the plasma membrane.

questions.text: A secretory vesicle fuses with the plasma membrane to release its protein by exocytosis.
questions.explanation: Exocytosis occurs at the plasma membrane, regardless of whether a cell wall is present.
questions.studyTip: Path: ribosome to rough ER to Golgi to vesicle to membrane.

questions.text: When tryptophan (trp) is abundant, the trp operon switches on to make more trp-synthesising enzymes.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students confused the trp operon's logic and its graph axes; abundant trp represses the operon.
questions.studyTip: Lots of trp turns the operon OFF.

questions.text: High trp levels repress the trp operon, reducing production of trp-synthesising enzymes.
questions.explanation: trp acts as a corepressor, so the cell stops making what it already has enough of.
questions.studyTip: Negative feedback: end product switches off its own pathway.

questions.text: In a eukaryotic cell, both transcription and translation take place in the nucleus.
questions.explanation: Mislocating these steps is a recurring error in gene-expression questions.
questions.studyTip: mRNA is made in the nucleus, then read in the cytoplasm.

questions.text: In eukaryotes transcription occurs in the nucleus and translation occurs at ribosomes in the cytoplasm.
questions.explanation: mRNA is processed and exported from the nucleus before translation.
questions.studyTip: Nucleus: transcription. Cytoplasm/ribosome: translation.

questions.text: Glucose and oxygen are inputs (reactants) of photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: In 2025, options confused inputs and outputs; glucose and O₂ are products of photosynthesis.
questions.studyTip: In: CO₂ + water. Out: glucose + O₂.

questions.text: Glucose and oxygen are outputs of photosynthesis, while carbon dioxide and water are the inputs.
questions.explanation: Light energy drives the conversion of CO₂ and water into glucose and oxygen.
questions.studyTip: Reactants left, products right.

questions.text: Anaerobic fermentation in animals produces ethanol and carbon dioxide.
questions.explanation: In 2025, ethanol and CO₂ were wrongly attributed to animals; that is yeast fermentation.
questions.studyTip: Yeast: ethanol + CO₂. Animals: lactic acid.

questions.text: Anaerobic fermentation yields ethanol and CO₂ in yeast, but lactic acid in animal cells.
questions.explanation: Different organisms regenerate NAD⁺ by different fermentation pathways.
questions.studyTip: Match the product to the organism.

questions.text: The electron transport chain is located on the outer mitochondrial membrane.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students confused the outer membrane as the ETC site; it occurs on the inner membrane.
questions.studyTip: ETC = inner membrane (cristae).

questions.text: The electron transport chain occurs on the inner mitochondrial membrane, where the most ATP is produced.
questions.explanation: The folded inner membrane (cristae) holds the ETC and ATP synthase.
questions.studyTip: Inner membrane = most ATP.

questions.text: Glycolysis takes place inside the mitochondria.
questions.explanation: Glycolysis occurs in the cytosol, so a cell without mitochondria can still make some ATP this way.
questions.studyTip: Glycolysis is in the cytosol.

questions.text: Glycolysis occurs in the cytosol, while the Krebs cycle and electron transport chain occur in the mitochondria.
questions.explanation: Only the later, oxygen-dependent stages are inside the mitochondria.
questions.studyTip: Cytosol: glycolysis. Matrix: Krebs. Inner membrane: ETC.

concepts.title: Name the enzyme: Rubisco fixes carbon

questions.text: It is the plant cell in general that fixes carbon during photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: In 2025, answers needed to specify that Rubisco fixes carbon dioxide and oxygen, not 'the plant' or 'carbon'.
questions.studyTip: Be specific: Rubisco does the fixing.

questions.text: The enzyme Rubisco fixes carbon dioxide (and can also bind oxygen), rather than 'the plant' fixing 'carbon'.
questions.explanation: Precision matters: it is CO₂ and O₂, fixed by a named enzyme.
questions.studyTip: Rubisco + CO₂, not 'plant' + 'carbon'.

concepts.title: C4 plants fix CO2 more efficiently

questions.text: C4 plants are adapted to take up more carbon dioxide than C3 plants.
questions.explanation: In 2024, 'take up more CO₂' was marked wrong; the adaptation is more efficient fixation.
questions.studyTip: C4 = better fixation, not more uptake.

questions.text: C4 plants are adapted to fix CO₂ more efficiently, concentrating it around Rubisco.
questions.explanation: C4 anatomy and biochemistry reduce photorespiration by raising CO₂ at the fixation site.
questions.studyTip: C4 concentrates CO₂ for Rubisco.

concepts.title: Mesophyll and bundle-sheath are different cells

questions.text: In C4 plants the bundle-sheath and mesophyll are different regions within a single cell or chloroplast.
questions.explanation: A common 2024 misconception placed bundle sheath and mesophyll inside one cell or chloroplast.
questions.studyTip: They are two separate cell types.

questions.text: In C4 plants, mesophyll cells and bundle-sheath cells are separate cells with a division of labour.
questions.explanation: Initial CO₂ capture (mesophyll) and the Calvin cycle (bundle sheath) occur in different cells.
questions.studyTip: C4 = spatial separation across two cell types.

questions.text: C4 plants close their stomata during the day to avoid photorespiration.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students confused C4 with CAM; daytime stomatal closing at night is the CAM strategy.
questions.studyTip: Don't mix up C4 and CAM.

questions.text: CAM plants open stomata at night, whereas C4 plants use spatial separation rather than daytime stomatal closing.
questions.explanation: CAM separates carbon capture in time; C4 separates it in space.
questions.studyTip: CAM = time (night). C4 = space (two cells).

questions.text: When CO₂ fixation plateaus as light intensity increases, light has become the limiting factor.
questions.explanation: In 2024, light was wrongly named as limiting at the plateau, and many explained the plateau but not the initial rise.
questions.studyTip: A plateau means something ELSE now limits the rate.

questions.text: If CO₂ fixation plateaus while light keeps increasing, a factor other than light has become limiting.
questions.explanation: Rate rises with a factor only while that factor is limiting; the plateau signals a new limit.
questions.studyTip: Explain both the rise and why it levels off.

concepts.title: Cytotoxic T and NK cells target infected cells

questions.text: Cytotoxic T cells and natural killer cells destroy pathogens such as bacteria and viruses directly.
questions.explanation: In 2022, many students said these cells destroy the pathogen rather than the body's infected cell.
questions.studyTip: They kill INFECTED CELLS, not free pathogens.

questions.text: Cytotoxic T cells and NK cells induce apoptosis in the body's own infected cells, not the pathogen directly.
questions.explanation: By killing infected host cells they remove the factories the pathogen is using.
questions.studyTip: Target = the infected self-cell.

questions.text: Antibodies are the molecules that activate helper T cells and other immune cells.
questions.explanation: In 2024, an option was wrong because it had antibodies (not cytokines) activating a cell type.
questions.studyTip: Cytokines are the signalling molecules.

questions.text: Cytokines, not antibodies, are the signalling molecules that activate many immune cells.
questions.explanation: Cytokines coordinate the immune response; antibodies bind antigens.
questions.studyTip: Cytokines signal; antibodies tag.

questions.text: The adaptive immune response is the same regardless of whether a pathogen is inside or outside body cells.
questions.explanation: In 2025, many students did not understand how responses differ for extracellular vs intracellular pathogens.
questions.studyTip: Location of the pathogen changes the response.

questions.text: Adaptive responses differ for extracellular and intracellular pathogens (humoral/antibody vs cell-mediated).
questions.explanation: Antibodies handle pathogens outside cells; cytotoxic T cells deal with infected cells.
questions.studyTip: Outside cells: antibodies. Inside cells: Tc cells.

questions.text: An extracellular pathogen is one that has not yet entered the body.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students confused extracellular pathogens with pathogens outside the body and with the lines of defence.
questions.studyTip: Extracellular = in the body, outside cells.

questions.text: An extracellular pathogen is inside the body but outside host cells, such as in blood or tissue fluid.
questions.explanation: Being extracellular is about position relative to cells, not whether it has entered the body.
questions.studyTip: In the body, between the cells.

concepts.title: Mothers pass antibodies, not memory cells

questions.text: A mother passes memory cells to her baby, giving the baby long-lasting active immunity.
questions.explanation: In 2025, responses wrongly described memory cells being passed from mother to child.
questions.studyTip: What's passed on is antibodies.

questions.text: A mother passes antibodies (across the placenta and in breast milk), giving the baby short-lived passive immunity.
questions.explanation: Receiving ready-made antibodies is passive immunity and fades as they break down.
questions.studyTip: Passive = borrowed antibodies = temporary.

concepts.title: Antibodies tag, they don't kill directly

questions.text: Antibodies destroy pathogens directly by breaking open their cell membranes.
questions.explanation: The 2023 report referenced agglutination, neutralisation and opsonisation as antibody actions.
questions.studyTip: Antibodies flag targets for other cells.

questions.text: Antibodies bind antigens to neutralise, agglutinate or opsonise pathogens, marking them for destruction.
questions.explanation: Other components (phagocytes, complement) carry out the actual destruction.
questions.studyTip: Bind and mark, then phagocytes finish the job.

questions.text: Antigenic drift involves sudden major changes to surface antigens that create a new pathogen.
questions.explanation: In 2024, drift and shift were confused; a major sudden change is shift, not drift.
questions.studyTip: Drift = gradual; shift = sudden and major.

questions.text: Antigenic drift is gradual minor change in antigens, while antigenic shift is a major sudden change.
questions.explanation: Drift slowly erodes existing immunity; shift can produce a pathogen the population has no immunity to.
questions.studyTip: Small drift over time; big shift all at once.

questions.text: Antibiotic resistance in existing bacteria is an example of the emergence of a new pathogen.
questions.explanation: In 2024, antibiotic resistance was not accepted as 'emergence of a new pathogen'.
questions.studyTip: Resistance changes an existing pathogen.

questions.text: Antibiotic resistance is change within an existing pathogen, not the emergence of a new one.
questions.explanation: A genuinely new or emerging pathogen is one the population has not encountered before.
questions.studyTip: Old pathogen changing is not a new pathogen.

concepts.title: Mutation makes alleles; selection moves them

questions.text: Natural selection and gene flow create the new alleles that account for DNA differences between groups.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students named processes that change allele frequencies when asked what produced the new DNA differences.
questions.studyTip: Mutation is the source of new alleles.

questions.text: Mutation creates new alleles, while selection, drift and gene flow change their frequencies.
questions.explanation: Only mutation introduces new genetic variation; the others redistribute what already exists.
questions.studyTip: Mutation MAKES; selection/drift/flow MOVE.

questions.text: Organisms adapt to their environment during their lifetime and pass those acquired changes to offspring.
questions.explanation: In 2024, Lamarckian statements (e.g. 'the trees adapted to high phosphorus') were penalised.
questions.studyTip: Acquired traits are not inherited.

questions.text: Selection acts on existing heritable variation; traits acquired during an organism's life are not inherited.
questions.explanation: Variation arises first (by mutation); the environment then selects among it.
questions.studyTip: Variation first, selection second - never 'tried to adapt'.

questions.text: The founder effect occurs when a population is drastically reduced by a disaster, leaving few survivors.
questions.explanation: In 2025, the bottleneck effect was described when the founder effect was required.
questions.studyTip: Founder = a few start a new population.

questions.text: The founder effect is a small group starting a new population; a bottleneck is a sharp population crash.
questions.explanation: Both reduce genetic diversity, but by different events.
questions.studyTip: Founder: few LEAVE. Bottleneck: most DIE.

concepts.title: How speciation occurs vs why species differ

questions.text: Explaining why two populations are now classified as separate species answers a question about how speciation occurred.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students explained why species were different instead of how speciation occurred.
questions.studyTip: 'How' wants the process.

questions.text: Describing how speciation occurred requires the process: isolation, divergence, then reproductive isolation.
questions.explanation: The mechanism (e.g. a barrier, then independent change) is what a 'how' question rewards.
questions.studyTip: Isolate, diverge, can no longer interbreed.

questions.text: Once two populations are geographically separated, no further explanation is needed for how they become genetically different.
questions.explanation: In 2024, pre-planned speciation answers did not address what caused genetic diversity between already-separate groups.
questions.studyTip: Separation alone isn't the mechanism.

questions.text: Separated populations diverge because different mutations, selection pressures and drift act on them independently.
questions.explanation: Isolation stops gene flow, but it is the independent genetic changes that drive divergence.
questions.studyTip: Different mutations + different selection in each group.

questions.text: When comparing populations of the same species, you should discuss how molecular homology distinguishes different species.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students used species-comparison responses for different populations of one finch species.
questions.studyTip: Populations are not species - read carefully.

questions.text: A question about populations of one species needs population-level reasoning, not a standard 'comparing species' answer.
questions.explanation: Applying a memorised species-comparison response to populations misses what is asked.
questions.studyTip: Match the answer to the level: population or species.

questions.text: On a phylogenetic tree, two species joined by a more distant (older) node are the most closely related.
questions.explanation: In 2024, relatedness questions required reading the most recent shared ancestor from the tree.
questions.studyTip: Closest relatives share the most RECENT node.

questions.text: Two species are most closely related when they share the most recent common ancestor (the nearest branch point).
questions.explanation: The more recently lineages diverged, the more closely related they are.
questions.studyTip: Recent split = close relatives.

questions.text: Homo sapiens and Neanderthals coexisted and interbred in Africa.
questions.explanation: The 2023 report noted H. sapiens originated in Africa and did not coexist with Neanderthals there.
questions.studyTip: Neanderthals did not live in Africa.

questions.text: Neanderthals did not live in Africa, so they did not coexist with the first H. sapiens there.
questions.explanation: Interbreeding occurred later, outside Africa, where the ranges overlapped.
questions.studyTip: Overlap (and interbreeding) happened outside Africa.

questions.text: In CRISPR-Cas9, the PAM site is part of the guide RNA that locates the target gene.
questions.explanation: In 2024, the roles of sgRNA, Cas9 endonuclease and the PAM site were poorly understood.
questions.studyTip: PAM is a short DNA sequence, not part of the sgRNA.

questions.text: The sgRNA guides the complex to a complementary DNA sequence, Cas9 cuts the DNA, and the PAM is a short DNA sequence beside the target.
questions.explanation: Each component has a distinct role: recognition (sgRNA), cutting (Cas9), and the adjacent PAM in the DNA.
questions.studyTip: sgRNA finds, Cas9 cuts, PAM sits beside (in the DNA).

questions.text: The Cas9 protein is produced by transcription.
questions.explanation: In 2024, an option was incorrect because it referred to Cas9 proteins being transcribed.
questions.studyTip: Proteins are translated.

questions.text: Cas9 is a protein, so it is produced by translation; transcription only makes RNA.
questions.explanation: Transcription makes mRNA from DNA; ribosomes then translate mRNA into the Cas9 protein.
questions.studyTip: Only RNA is transcribed; proteins are translated.

questions.text: To transform an organism, the bacterium's plasmid genes are inserted into the target organism's genome.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students reversed the direction: the cress gene was inserted into the Agrobacterium plasmid, then into canola.
questions.studyTip: Gene of interest goes INTO the plasmid.

questions.text: A gene of interest is inserted into a plasmid, which is then taken up by the host so the host expresses that gene.
questions.explanation: The plasmid is the vector that carries the chosen gene into the host.
questions.studyTip: Gene to plasmid to host - mind the direction.

questions.text: Larger DNA fragments travel further through the gel than smaller fragments.
questions.explanation: In 2024, a restriction enzyme failing to cut left a larger fragment that travelled less far.
questions.studyTip: Smaller fragments move further.

questions.text: Smaller DNA fragments travel further through the gel, while larger fragments move more slowly and stay near the wells.
questions.explanation: The gel matrix slows large fragments more, separating DNA by size.
questions.studyTip: Small = fast = far; large = slow = near the top.

questions.text: DNA is positively charged, so it moves through the gel towards the cathode (the negative electrode).
questions.explanation: In 2024, 'anode' and 'cathode' and their charges were often confused.
questions.studyTip: DNA is negative; it runs to the positive anode.

questions.text: DNA is negatively charged, so it migrates towards the anode (the positive electrode).
questions.explanation: The phosphate backbone gives DNA a negative charge, pulling it to the positive end.
questions.studyTip: Negative DNA to positive anode.

questions.text: To make human insulin, both insulin chains' genes are inserted together into a single plasmid.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students commonly misunderstood that the insulin genes are inserted separately, not together.
questions.studyTip: Each chain gene goes in its own plasmid.

questions.text: The two insulin chain genes are inserted separately into their own plasmids and expressed in separate cultures.
questions.explanation: The chains are produced independently and then combined to form functional insulin.
questions.studyTip: Separate plasmids, separate cultures, then combine.
